title: Drop scones
subtitle: Scottish griddle pancakes made from a thick batter cooked on a hot griddle
excerpt: Small, thick Scottish pancakes made from a simple batter of flour, egg, milk, and a little sugar, dropped onto a hot greased griddle or heavy frying pan. They puff up as they cook and are ready to flip when bubbles rise and pop on the surface. Eaten warm with butter and jam. Also called Scotch pancakes. Quick to make and cooked entirely on the hob with no oven required.
sourceNotes: Drop scones (Scotch pancakes) are documented in Scottish household cookery literature from the nineteenth century. F Marian McNeill, The Scots Kitchen (1929), describes them as a traditional griddle preparation made from a thick batter dropped onto a hot girdle (griddle). The combination of flour, egg, buttermilk or sweet milk, and baking powder cooked on a dry or lightly greased griddle is consistent across Scottish cookery collections of the period.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze cold drop scones in a sealed bag for up to 1 month. Toast from frozen or warm in a dry pan.
recipe.batchNotes: Double or triple the batter and cook in batches. Keep cooked scones warm wrapped in a clean tea towel.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten the day they are made. Day-old drop scones can be refreshed in a dry pan over low heat or toasted.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: cooked on a hot griddle or heavy frying pan over medium to medium-high heat; no oven required. The surface should be hot enough that a small drop of batter sets within 10 seconds.

glossaryTerms.definition: Drop scones are ready to flip when bubbles have formed across the surface and the edges of each scone look dry and set rather than wet and shiny. The bubbles rise through the batter as it sets, and when they begin to burst and leave small holes rather than filling back in, the underside is cooked and the scone can be flipped. Flipping too early produces a pale, doughy underside; waiting until the surface is fully set before flipping produces an even, golden result. After flipping, the second side takes about half the time of the first.

content.text: The key skill is patience: wait for the
bubbles to form and pop on the surface
before flipping. A heavy cast-iron frying pan holds heat evenly and gives a better result than a thin non-stick pan. Keep the heat at medium once the pan is hot.

attrs.prepNote: melted, plus extra for greasing the pan

content.text: Sift the flour, baking powder, sugar, and salt into a bowl. Make a well in the centre and add the egg and half the milk. Whisk from the centre outward until the batter is smooth and thick. Stir in the melted butter, then add the remaining milk gradually until the batter just drops from a spoon in thick, slow blobs. It should be thicker than a crepe batter.

Heat a heavy frying pan or griddle over medium heat. Grease it lightly with butter and wipe off any excess with a folded piece of kitchen paper. Drop heaped tablespoon-sized portions of batter onto the pan, spacing them well apart. Cook until bubbles rise and pop across the surface and the edges look set, about 2 minutes. Flip with a palette knife or fish slice and cook the second side for 1 minute until golden. Keep warm wrapped in a clean tea towel. Serve with butter and jam.
